0.title: Knowledge Hub Foundations
0.abstract: Knowledge Hub Foundations curates the first expansion wave of Electronic Artefacts knowledge records around graph systems, preservation, generative systems, signal, audio and public semantic publishing.

1.title: Knowledge Hub Second Wave
1.abstract: Knowledge Hub Second Wave groups a larger expansion of Electronic Artefacts knowledge records across hypertext, open source, metadata, creative coding, web audio, motion, typography, HCI, algorithmic music and generative AI.

2.title: Algorithmic Composition
2.definition: Algorithmic composition is the use of rules, procedures, probability, computation or formal systems to generate or organize musical material.
2.abstract: Algorithmic composition connects music theory, computation, cybernetics, generative systems and electronic music by treating composition as the design of procedures as well as sounds.

3.title: Augmented Intelligence
3.definition: Augmented intelligence is the design of tools, interfaces and systems that expand human perception, reasoning, memory and creative capability.
3.abstract: Augmented intelligence reframes computation as a partner in human work, linking HCI, AI, knowledge systems, creative tools and Electronic Artefacts runtime research.

4.title: Contextual Execution
4.definition: Contextual execution is the practice of making identity, state, permissions, relationships and operating context active constraints on what a system can do.
4.abstract: Contextual execution describes runtime behavior where an operation is evaluated through entity identity, graph relations, permissions, temporal state and intended scope.

5.title: Creative Coding
5.definition: Creative coding is the use of programming as an artistic, design or exploratory medium rather than only as an engineering implementation tool.
5.abstract: Creative coding connects software, visual culture, sound, interaction, generative systems, education and experimental publishing.

6.title: Cybernetic Feedback
6.definition: Cybernetic feedback is the circulation of information about a system's state back into the system so that behavior can be regulated, adapted or transformed.
6.abstract: Cybernetic feedback connects control, communication, systems theory, generative art, machine listening, adaptive interfaces and runtime governance.

7.title: Digital Preservation
7.definition: Digital preservation is the long-term work of keeping digital objects understandable, authentic, usable and accessible despite changes in formats, software, hardware and context.
7.abstract: Digital preservation connects file formats, metadata, provenance, storage, rights, migration and interpretation into a long-term care practice for digital culture.

8.title: Entity Identity
8.definition: Entity identity is the durable naming of a person, concept, project, program, publication or artefact so that references remain stable as descriptions evolve.
8.abstract: Entity identity gives knowledge systems a way to cite things without confusing names, pages, files, versions and changing descriptions.

9.title: Generative AI
9.definition: Generative AI refers to machine-learning systems that produce or transform media such as text, image, audio, video, code or structured data from learned patterns.
9.abstract: Generative AI connects machine learning, creative workflows, latent spaces, risk management, provenance, authorship and cultural production.

10.title: Generative System
10.definition: A generative system is a rule-based, procedural or computational arrangement that produces variable outputs from defined constraints, materials and processes.
10.abstract: Generative systems connect creative coding, cybernetics, algorithmic composition, simulation, design tools and AI by shifting authorship toward rules, feedback and selection.

11.title: Graph Runtime
11.definition: A graph runtime is an execution environment in which addressable entities and typed relationships participate directly in contextual computation.
11.abstract: Electronic Artefacts uses graph runtime to describe systems where entities, relationships, identity, context and events form an executable structure rather than a passive data model.

12.title: Human Computer Interaction
12.definition: Human computer interaction is the study and design of relationships between people, computational systems, interfaces, tasks, environments and cultures of use.
12.abstract: HCI connects interface design, accessibility, creative tools, augmented intelligence, software ergonomics and the social consequences of computation.

13.title: Hypertext
13.definition: Hypertext is a system of non-linear text and media connected by links, allowing readers and machines to traverse knowledge through associations.
13.abstract: Hypertext is a foundational concept for the web, digital archives, knowledge graphs, digital gardens, public notes and Electronic Artefacts knowledge publishing.

14.title: Internet Culture
14.definition: Internet culture is the set of social forms, aesthetics, protocols, communities, memes, archives and practices that emerge through networked communication.
14.abstract: Internet culture connects network protocols, web publishing, platform communities, memes, archives, open source, digital folklore and public knowledge systems.

15.title: Knowledge Graph
15.definition: A knowledge graph is a structured network of entities, identifiers and typed relationships that makes knowledge navigable, queryable and reusable across contexts.
15.abstract: Electronic Artefacts uses knowledge graph to describe public knowledge systems where concepts, projects, programs, publications, sources and artefacts keep stable identities and typed relations.

16.title: Linked Data
16.definition: Linked Data is a web publishing approach that identifies things with stable URIs and connects them through machine-readable statements.
16.abstract: Linked Data gives public knowledge systems a way to make entities, pages, references and datasets interoperable across sites and software systems.

17.title: Metadata
17.definition: Metadata is structured information about a resource, such as title, creator, date, format, source, rights, subject, relation and preservation context.
17.abstract: Metadata is the descriptive, administrative, structural and preservation layer that makes archives, catalogues, digital objects and knowledge graphs usable over time.

18.title: Motion Design
18.definition: Motion design is the use of time, movement, transition, rhythm and sequencing to structure visual communication and interface behavior.
18.abstract: Motion design connects visual culture, interface semantics, typography, animation, sound, choreography and web interaction.

19.title: Open Source
19.definition: Open source is a software and cultural practice in which source code is made available under licenses that allow use, study, modification and redistribution.
19.abstract: Open source is both a licensing model and a cultural infrastructure for software, creative tools, public knowledge, maintainership and technical education.

20.title: Personal Knowledge System
20.definition: A personal knowledge system is a practice and tool environment for capturing, linking, revising and retrieving notes, sources, ideas and decisions over time.
20.abstract: Personal knowledge systems connect digital gardens, wikis, note archives, research methods, backlinks and long-term intellectual memory.

21.title: Procedural Graphics
21.definition: Procedural graphics are images or visual systems generated from rules, algorithms, parameters, simulation or code rather than only manually drawn assets.
21.abstract: Procedural graphics connect shaders, generative systems, motion design, creative coding, visual culture and real-time web interfaces.

22.title: Provenance
22.definition: Provenance is information about the people, activities, sources, transformations and decisions involved in producing a thing or piece of data.
22.abstract: Provenance helps readers assess reliability, rights, authorship, lineage, context and trust across research, archive, software and cultural records.

23.title: Signal Archaeology
23.definition: Signal archaeology is the interpretation of technical, sonic, visual or archival traces as evidence of hidden processes, transformations and histories.
23.abstract: Signal archaeology gives Electronic Artefacts a way to connect audio analysis, media residue, archival provenance, memory and cultural interpretation.

24.title: Typography
24.definition: Typography is the design and arrangement of written language through typefaces, spacing, hierarchy, rhythm, scale and reading conditions.
24.abstract: Typography connects design, reading systems, accessibility, visual culture, web interfaces and long-form knowledge publishing.

25.title: Web Audio
25.definition: Web audio is the use of browser-based audio APIs and related web technologies to synthesize, process, analyze, route and present sound.
25.abstract: Web audio connects sound design, browser runtimes, interactive media, creative coding and public sound tools.

26.title: Electronic Artefacts
26.abstract: Electronic Artefacts is an independent creative technology studio, software laboratory, research organization, cultural platform, label and publishing system.

27.title: ORETH
27.abstract: ORETH is an Electronic Artefacts audio intelligence and artistic research program concerned with signal analysis, pattern detection, memory, machine listening and experimental music production.

28.title: VASTE
28.abstract: VASTE is the primary proprietary runtime developed and commercialized by Electronic Artefacts for graph architectures, contextual execution, identity systems and modular world construction.

29.title: Palimpsests
29.abstract: Palimpsests is an Electronic Artefacts cultural project and conceptual album cycle connected to ORETH, memory, inheritance, transmission, entropy, signal and layered artistic production.

30.title: Vestiges
30.abstract: Vestiges is a VASTE-powered knowledge infrastructure platform designed to map, preserve, connect, transmit and activate human know-how.

31.title: Algorithmic Composition and Rule-Based Music
31.abstract: This article explains algorithmic composition through rules, stochastic methods, cybernetics, electronic music, creative coding, generative systems and ORETH.

32.title: Contextual Execution and Graph Runtimes
32.abstract: This article explains contextual execution as a runtime principle for systems where identity, relations, permissions and state influence what operations can occur.

33.title: Creative Coding Pedagogy from Logo to p5.js
33.abstract: This article explains creative coding pedagogy through exploratory programming, computational literacy, Processing, p5.js, visual feedback and open learning communities.

34.title: Digital Preservation and Living Archives
34.abstract: This article explains digital preservation as an active cultural practice that keeps digital objects, metadata, provenance and interpretation usable over time.

35.title: Foundational Lineage #001
35.abstract: This research note records early architecture, communication-system and documentation threads that led to Electronic Artefacts, Runtime Theory and VASTE.

36.title: Generative AI, Latent Space and Creative Workflows
36.abstract: This article explains generative AI as a creative workflow topic, covering latent spaces, prompts, provenance, risk, evaluation, authorship and Electronic Artefacts systems.

37.title: Generative Systems, Cybernetics and Creative Coding
37.abstract: This article traces generative systems from cybernetic feedback and algorithmic art to creative coding, sound design and contemporary AI-assisted production.

38.title: Human Computer Interaction for Creative Tools
38.abstract: This article explains HCI for creative tools, focusing on feedback, agency, flow, error recovery, accessibility, augmented intelligence and computational media.

39.title: Hypertext and Augmented Knowledge Systems
39.abstract: This article explains hypertext as a long-term knowledge architecture, connecting Vannevar Bush, Douglas Engelbart, web links, knowledge graphs and augmented intelligence.

40.title: Knowledge Graphs for Cultural Infrastructure
40.abstract: This article explains how knowledge graphs support cultural infrastructure by giving people, works, sources, concepts, projects and archives stable identities and explicit relations.

41.title: Linked Data and Public Knowledge Pages
41.abstract: This article explains how public knowledge pages can use stable URLs, JSON-LD, RDF-oriented identifiers and internal links to become useful to humans, search engines and AI systems.

42.title: Metadata, Cataloguing and Cultural Memory
42.abstract: This article explains metadata as a cultural memory layer, connecting cataloguing, Dublin Core, PREMIS, METS, provenance, archives and knowledge graphs.

43.title: Motion Design, Time and Interface Semantics
43.abstract: This article explains motion design as a semantic layer for digital interfaces, connecting time, transition, attention, accessibility and Web Animations.

44.title: Open Source as Cultural Infrastructure
44.abstract: This article frames open source as cultural infrastructure, explaining licenses, maintenance, creative tools, dependency chains, commons and public knowledge systems.

45.title: Personal Knowledge Systems and Digital Gardens
45.abstract: This article explains personal knowledge systems and digital gardens as durable practices for notes, sources, backlinks, revision, public learning and research libraries.

46.title: Procedural Graphics, Shaders and Visual Systems
46.abstract: This article explains procedural graphics through rules, shaders, parameters, WebGL, generative systems and visual grammars for digital art and interfaces.

47.title: Signal Archaeology, Audio Memory and Machine Listening
47.abstract: This article explains signal archaeology as a way to read sonic and technical traces through audio analysis, provenance, memory and machine listening.

48.title: Typography, Reading Systems and Digital Interfaces
48.abstract: This article explains typography as infrastructure for digital reading, connecting type, layout, hierarchy, accessibility, CSS Fonts and long-form knowledge publishing.

49.title: Web Audio and Browser-Based Sound Systems
49.abstract: This article explains Web Audio as a browser-native sound architecture for synthesis, processing, analysis, interactive essays, creative coding and public audio tools.

50.title: Runtime Theory
50.abstract: Runtime Theory investigates the minimum identities, relations, contexts and event primitives required for a coherent executable system.

51.title: ActivityPub
51.abstract: ActivityPub is a decentralized social networking protocol standardized by W3C for federated server-to-server and client-to-server activity exchange.

52.title: CSS Fonts
52.abstract: CSS Fonts defines web font selection, font faces and typographic behavior for digital interfaces and web documents.

53.title: JSON-LD
53.abstract: JSON-LD is a W3C format for serializing Linked Data in JSON, making semantic graph metadata compatible with common web programming workflows.

54.title: RDF
54.abstract: RDF is a W3C graph data model for representing information as subject-predicate-object statements about resources.

55.title: Web Animations
55.abstract: Web Animations defines a timing and animation model for changes to web page presentation and related programming interfaces.

56.title: Web Audio API
56.abstract: The Web Audio API is a browser platform technology for routing, synthesizing, processing and analyzing audio through connected audio nodes.

57.title: WebGL
57.abstract: WebGL is a web platform technology for rendering interactive 2D and 3D graphics in compatible browsers using GPU acceleration.
